text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic chordate lobectomy by taking down the ligamentum teres and falciform ligament to retract the left lobe of the liver. The left triangular ligament is dissected up towards the inferior vena cava, allowing for the placement of a Nathanson liver retractor. The veil of tissue over the enlarged chordate lobe is visible, with the replaced left hepatic artery seen flopping over the top.

The surgeon dissects around the capsule of the liver and the caudate lobe, moving towards the base of the lesser omentum. Large accessory vessels are visible, and the Thunderbeat device is used for dissection and blood sealing. The dissection proceeds behind the insertion of the left hepatic vein into the inferior vena cava and along the front of the caudate lobe, joining the left lobe of the liver in the groove for the ligamentum venosum. Areolar tissue is removed around the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon extends the dissection of fibrous tissue between the chordate and the left lateral section to achieve a medial margin. The portacaval shunt is visible below the chordate lobe, with a tongue of chordate tissue surrounding it. The inferior vena cava comes into view, and small chordate vessels on the cava are controlled with bipolar diathermy to stop bleeding. Further dissection reveals short gastric and hepatic vessels from the chordate lobe entering the inferior vena cava, which are secured with haemoclips.

The laparoscope provides a clear view along the length of the inferior vena cava, allowing identification and clipping of small vessels after dissection. Bipolar diathermy is used to control the chordate side before cutting, minimizing clip usage. Small tributaries from the hilum to the chordate lobe are divided with bipolar diathermy, feeding the visibly enlarged adenoma in the chordate lobe. Dissection of the lesser omentum continues on the undersurface of the replaced hepatic artery, with larger vessels from the portal venous structures in the hilum being controlled and dissected.

The surgeon dissects alongside the shunt, identifying and controlling small tributaries running to the caudate lobe using bipolar diathermy and the harmonic function of the Thunderbeat. Blunt dissection is employed to develop tissue planes, minimizing parenchymal destruction.

The surgery is being performed using an Olympus three-dimensional laparoscope with glasses that give three-dimensional type view. It's a very comfortable way of observing what's going on and in some ways may help with the dissection and recognition of structures and certainly helps with suturing and a feeling of depth perception that can only be gained through three-D imaging. The other advantage of the three-dimensional scope for this type of an operation is that it does have a great deal of flexibility once it's inside the abdomen. It can turn around almost to ninety degrees allowing the camera to be placed out of the way of the instruments and yet give a great view.

The surgeon continues dissection along the inferior vena cava, identifying and ligating substantial tributaries from the chordate lobe. The inferior vena cava is dissected off the back of the chordate lobe, with attempts to pass a tape from behind to visualize the shunt from the front. Further dissection reveals a small branch from the shunt to the chordate lobe, which is taken down to elongate the shunt. A tape is passed anteriorly, and dissection reveals a developing window of areola tissue, indicating the shunt's position below.

The surgeon uses tape for traction while dissecting near the hepatic artery to control inflow to the chordate lobe, peeling it away from vital structures. A small bleeder from the back of the hepatic artery is identified and preserved. Dissection continues into the bridge of liver tissue as the chordate crosses over to the main liver, ensuring an adequate margin. A venous bleeder is packed off with a Raytech to control bleeding. The chordate lobe appears ischemic, changing to a pale color as blood flow ceases.

The surgeon performs dissection in front of the inferior vena cava and behind the left hepatic vein, revealing the common trunk of the middle hepatic vein as it inserts into the cava. Using a laparoscope, the superior attachments of the caudate lobe are divided, despite challenges posed by the replaced hepatic artery. The Thunder Pete is used to dissect and seal vessels in the bridge of tissue over the front of the shunt, addressing a small vein with bipolar diathermy.

The surgeon carefully places tape to avoid damaging the underlying shunt, allowing elevation of the caudate lobe at a different angle to approach its thinning attachments in front of the inferior vena cava. Small vessels remain attached between the porta hepatis and the caudate lobe. The sucker is used to manage minor oozing and serves as a retractor and blunt dissector alongside the Thunderbead energy device. A thin bridge of tissue runs in front of the inferior vena cava, and mobilization continues to prepare for its division with a stapling device.

The surgeon controls a small vessel by placing a tie around it, followed by a Hem-o-lok clip. The Thunder Beat is used to seal the opposite side due to limited space. The operation focuses on the caudate lobe, with the last attachments being taken back towards the porta hepatis. A stapler is used on a small pedicle, ensuring no clips are caught inside to prevent misfire and potential bleeding.

The surgeon divides a capsule at the back of the caudate lobe, thinning out the tissue and revealing the bouncing inferior vena cava behind it. A fibrous structure resembling a small vessel is clipped with a hemlock clip. The caudate lobe is detached, and the hepatic artery remains patent. A bleeding point on the parenchymal surface is packed temporarily while the specimen is placed in a bag and removed through a small Pfannenstiel incision. After closing the incision, the packing is removed to address the bleeding area.

The surgeon sutures a bleeding point deep within the surgical field, using a needle to cinch the suture around the area. Despite the appearance of blood, the bleeding is minimal. Irrigation is performed, and fibrillar is applied to aid clot formation, particularly near the inferior vena cava. The liver appears abnormal in color, indicating ischemia.
